title: Slow Growth For IT Spending In 2003
author: Gregg Keizer, Techweb News / InformationWeek
abstract: InformationWeek article (Jan 3 2003) by Gregg Keizer summarizing Aberdeen Group's 2003 top tech trends forecast. Peter Kastner, Aberdeen's chief research officer, is the primary source: global IT spending up 4% in 2003 (up from <1% in 2002), back-end-loaded; outsourcing on-demand is the bright spot, driving deals like IBM-Deutsche Bank ($2.5B) and IBM-J.P. Morgan Chase ($5B); Kastner troubled by offshore outsourcing, calling it 'a horse of a completely different color and a potential economic threat to the livelihood of a lot of Americans.' Linux server growth 40% in 2003 (down from 50%+ in 2002); Linux desktop will still not break out and at best will overtake Apple at ~3% of enterprise market. Key Kastner aphorism: 'Ultimately, psychology and not technology will determine the growth rate for IT spending this year.'
importance_rationale: Canonical Aberdeen/Kastner 2003 forecast — baseline data point for reconstructing the post-dot-com recession recovery trajectory; combines IT spending, outsourcing, and Linux predictions.
relevance_rationale: Specific 2003 forecast numbers are dated, but Kastner's on-demand outsourcing framing and offshoring-as-macroeconomic-threat thesis remain live analytical categories.
prescience_rationale: (1) IT-services consolidation via on-demand outsourcing validated: IBM, HP/EDS, Accenture all grew managed-services lines aggressively. (2) Offshoring-as-economic-threat framing was empirically borne out in US IT employment patterns 2003-2015. (3) Linux-desktop-will-not-break-out prediction confirmed: Linux desktop never exceeded ~3-4% through 2020s despite Ubuntu/Chromebook. (4) 4% IT spending growth estimate for 2003 was broadly close to actual.
